text: My name is Mark Glover from Austin, Texas. This is a video of a robotic unoscolar hernia that was present for approximately seven to eight years prior to surgery.

The surgeon laparoscopically reduces the chronically incarcerated ascending and transverse colon, along with a large amount of omentum, from the hernia. The procedure involves using the shaft of the instrument to apply lateral pressure without directly grabbing the bowel, while manual manipulation of the scrotum is performed at the bedside. After approximately forty-five minutes, the bowel is completely reduced, and the robot is docked to begin preperitoneal dissection.
The surgeon isolates the peritoneal sac from the cord structures and transects it after adequate distal dissection. The large hernia sac is visible, and the potential for seroma development is noted.

The video shows the peritoneal defect where the hernia sac was transected. A large heavyweight polypropylene mesh is being placed over the defect and secured in multiple locations with absorbable sutures.
The surgeon fixates the mesh in place and begins closing the peritoneum. After completing the peritoneal closure, the defect is closed, ensuring the mesh is excluded from the peritoneal cavity.
